gist: The Blockworks Research team dissects MicroStrategy's (Strategy) deteriorating credit position on its perpetual preferred 'Stretch' as Bitcoin breaks below 60K, then pivots to the breakout in onchain tokenized equity volume on Solana (X Stocks, Backpack), arguing tokenized equities are now out-trading memecoins and could become the dominant Solana volume category within a year. They surface specific equity plays (Jito/JTX, Radium, Meteora, Backpack) and Luke closes with a technical cycle-low call on Bitcoin.

insights.claim: MicroStrategy's perpetual preferred 'Stretch' is trading at roughly a 25% discount to par (around 75 vs par 100), implying the market demands a ~14-15% effective yield to hold paper that pays an 11.5% dividend on par.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Blown-out credit spread on Stretch is a real-time market signal of distress on Strategy's liability side and a potential forced-selling catalyst for Bitcoin.
insights.quote: at 75 basically the the market is saying we need a 14 15% effective yield to hold the paper. um while they're paying an 11 and a half% dividend yield on par value 100

insights.claim: Stretch outstanding supply has ballooned from about 1 billion a year ago to over 10 billion, growing the dividend obligation while Bitcoin's drawdown roughly halved the asset side, making Strategy's liability side far more concerning.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: The asset-liability mismatch is the core solvency risk; watch Stretch issuance pace and BTC price as the two variables that determine dividend coverage.
insights.quote: you continue to issue stretch um from from what was you know 1 billion a year ago up to over 10 billion in outstanding supply now uh that that liability side of the equation is just much more concerning

insights.claim: Strategy can only toggle two levers on Stretch — the dividend yield (price of money) or the supply (quantity) — and at a 25% discount to par the optimal move is to dump Bitcoin to buy back the paper cheaply, reducing the liability.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Frames the binary decision tree investors should monitor; a buyback via BTC sales would be bearish near-term for BTC but credit-positive for Strategy.
insights.quote: there's the price of money and then the quantity of it. And they could you know hike the dividend yield up to 15% ... or they can reduce the quantity

insights.claim: A large Bitcoin sale by Strategy to pay back Stretch would likely mark a capitulatory, near-term bottom moment, after which there is little left to fear.
insights.speaker: Carlos (guest)
insights.so_what: Identifies a forced-seller capitulation event as a potential buy signal for Bitcoin rather than a continuation of downside.
insights.quote: if you know strategy were to unload a large chunk of BTC that would probably mark some kind of capitulatory maybe even near-term bottom type of moment

insights.claim: Launching the perpetual preferred was a strategic error because it raised Strategy's cost of capital dramatically and the debt is perpetual, with no maturity or duration to hedge or manage around.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Challenges the bull narrative on Strategy's financing engineering; perpetual structure removes the natural escape hatch that convertibles offered.
insights.quote: I thought launching the perpetual preferred was perhaps one of the the stupidest things strategy has done. Uh, raised their cost of capital tremendously and the debt doesn't mature

insights.claim: Strategy's ATM premium is shrinking fast (from 1.14 to ~1.1 earlier this week), thinning the margin available to raise cash to defend Stretch without selling Bitcoin into a falling market.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: A collapsing ATM premium narrows Strategy's non-dilutive funding options, increasing the odds of BTC liquidation; track mNAV/premium as a leading indicator.
insights.quote: one's the ATM which is that premium is shrinking. It was 1.14 earlier this week. I think it dropped down to to 1.1

insights.claim: Strategy has shown willingness to sacrifice common holders for the sake of Bitcoin, convert holders, and Stretch holders, and is likely to keep tapping the ATM even in a thin premium window.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Implies MSTR common equity is structurally subordinated in management's priorities, a risk for common holders even if the credit survives.
insights.quote: increasingly it looks like uh strategies as they have been uh more than willing and able to sacrifice commoners um for the sake of both bitcoin than uh convert holders and um stretch

insights.claim: Convertible notes may be a superior financing tool to perpetual preferreds because dilution from converts at higher equity prices is accretive if cycle-timed well, whereas perpetuals must be paid in dollar terms forever.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Useful lens for evaluating any digital-asset-treasury financing stack; favors issuers using converts timed to cycle lows over perpetual-preferred issuers.
insights.quote: you get diluted um 100 200% higher on the commons like big deal ... whereas with the perpetual preferreds um you you got to you got to pay that in dollar terms eventually

insights.claim: BitMine's preferred-instrument position is healthier than Strategy's because its balance sheet is less encumbered by debt and convertible cliffs (Strategy faces large 2027+ cliffs) and ETH staking yield provides an income side to service obligations.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Differentiates digital-asset-treasury issuers by balance-sheet quality and yield; ETH-based DATs with staking income may be more durable than BTC perpetual-preferred issuers.
insights.quote: Bitmine's definitely in a bit better of a position where firstly like they don't have as encumbered a balance sheet um with like debt and outstanding convertible notes the way uh strategy does

insights.claim: The key risk for any perpetual-preferred issuer is over-issuance relative to the duration of coverage; the instrument has a legitimate place if issuers like BitMine avoid letting the dividend obligation grow too large.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Provides a checklist (issuance size vs coverage duration) for underwriting future DAT preferred issuance rather than dismissing the instrument outright.
insights.quote: the bigger problem was it got too big for strategies balance sheet and that that dividend obligation uh grew grew too large relative to uh the duration of coverage that they hold

insights.claim: Solana put up just under 1.4 billion in weekly spot tokenized equity volume, accounting for roughly 98% of total onchain spot equity volume across all chains.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Quantifies Solana's near-monopoly on onchain spot equities, supporting a re-rating thesis for SOL as the venue capturing this new flow.
insights.quote: Salana putting up about just short of 1.4 billion in weekly spot equity volume and this also accounts for like 98% of um total onchain spot equity volume across chains

insights.claim: Onchain equities are now trading more volume than memecoins on Solana, with tokenized equities near 20% of spot pair-category share versus memes around 10-15%.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: A category rotation from memes to equities signals a maturation of Solana flow that could justify a higher, more sustainable valuation multiple.
insights.quote: now it's nearly 20% memes look like they're bit under 10 like around 10 to 15

insights.claim: Tokenized equities could become the majority of Solana spot volume within a year as Backpack lists effectively the entire stock exchange and additional exchanges, re-rating Solana as a more serious 'house of spot' ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: Explicit one-year bull thesis on SOL tied to tokenized-equity listing expansion; the catalyst is breadth of listed names.
insights.quote: I wouldn't be surprised if the majority of share within a year becomes tokenized equities and I feel like that would rerate Salana as a more serious ecosystem

insights.claim: The tokenized equity boom is the downstream effect of the post-2024 favorable US regulatory climate, since compliant spot equities onchain were not feasible pre-2024 and issuers are now ramping as fast as compliance allows.
insights.speaker: Carlos (guest)
insights.so_what: Frames the boom as regulation-gated rather than demand-gated, implying continued ramp as more pieces fall into place rather than a transient fad.
insights.quote: I don't think spot equities onchain in current form could have existed really any sooner ... pre2024 that was not it wasn't an option to do it like this

insights.claim: Passage of the Clarity Act would unlock big institutional money and players who currently cannot touch the chain, and those players are more likely to trade equities than memecoins.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific legislative catalyst whose passage would expand the addressable institutional buyer base for onchain equities.
insights.quote: if we get the Clarity Act to pass, I feel like that's what's going to allow like big institutional money or players or traders to actually touch the chain, which they can't essentially do at the moment

insights.claim: Solana's equity surge mirrors hyperliquid markets where equity perp volume rose from 2-5% of OI to 40% within a quarter, suggesting the early innings of a similar S-curve on Solana.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Offers a base-rate analogue for how fast a new asset category can capture share, informing the speed of the Solana equity ramp.
insights.quote: I remember watching the early signs of uh equity volume on hyperlquid markets when there were I don't know two to 5% of volume in OI and then quickly rose to 40% within a quarter

insights.claim: Only about 3 tokenized stocks are live versus ~5,000 in the S&P and ~30,000 globally, so the tokenized equity market is in its very early days with enormous listing runway.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: Sizes the TAM gap (3 of 30,000 names), supporting a long-duration growth thesis for issuers and the underlying chain.
insights.quote: that's three out of there's 5,000 in S&P. There's around 30,000 around the globe. So there's lots to come

insights.claim: Issuers are winning by listing the highest-attention, highest-momentum names (SpaceX, Micron, SanDisk) when they are exciting, mirroring Hyperliquid's success with gold and oil, rather than broadly tokenizing the whole S&P.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: A go-to-market playbook for tokenized-asset issuers: prioritize speculative high-attention names where users lack offchain access, not broad index coverage.
insights.quote: this like immediate term strategy of just going after like the highest attention and impact assets makes more sense than just saying like, okay, we're going to tokenize everyone in the S&P 500

insights.claim: Backpack's tokenized equities are one-for-one fungible with real-world shares and redeemable to a brokerage without a taxable event, a legal structure superior to X Stocks' Swiss tracker certificate where the holder is a creditor rather than a shareholder.
insights.speaker: Carlos (guest)
insights.so_what: Structural differentiation among issuers determines institutional/whale adoption; Backpack's brokerage gateway is the key competitive moat.
insights.quote: I do think that backpack legal structure is superior in the sense that if you if you want to go to your brokerage, you can do that and you don't have to incur like a taxable event or whatever

insights.claim: Backpack's onchain-to-brokerage gateway matters more for market structure and market makers than for end users, because reconcilable cross-venue inventory enables tighter spreads and deeper liquidity, and large whales care about it more than everyday users.
insights.speaker: Carlos (guest)
insights.so_what: The real adoption driver is MM efficiency and whale demand, so liquidity quality (spreads) is the metric to watch, not retail UX.
insights.quote: it's also about uh market structure in the sense that if you can go from the onchain version of the token to to the brokerage version that's so much better for market makers and so much better for liquidity

insights.claim: Superior market-structure dynamics will let Backpack attract more prop market makers, eventually delivering the best pricing and potentially kicking out competing issuers, likely consolidating each asset into only one or two dominant versions.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: Predicts winner-take-most consolidation among tokenized-equity issuers driven by MM preference; favors the issuer with the best market structure.
insights.quote: Eventually, the best pricing will be on back stocks and that could eventually kick out other players um if they don't have as favorable market structure dynamics

insights.claim: Issuer competition will resolve like Hyperliquid's multiple deployers where one venue (HyperliquidX) wins the majority of volume and liquidity, so a couple of tokenized-equity issuers will accumulate most volume over time as the market awards trust.
insights.speaker: Carlos (guest)
insights.so_what: Reframes multi-issuer fragmentation as healthy competition (like Bitcoin ETF issuers) that naturally concentrates into a few winners; bet on liquidity leaders.
insights.quote: you'll have something similar to hyperlquid where there are multiple deployers but then trade XYZ wins the majority of volume and the majority of liquidity

themes.theme: DEX market structure: prop AMM vs pools

insights.claim: Prop AMMs dominate roughly 80-90% of backpack SpaceX volume (Zero Intl/Terrestrial, GoonFi cited), while generic pool models like Meteora retain only 10-20% share.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: Prop AMMs are capturing the liquid-equity flow, but pools' persistent minority share hints at residual value in the pool model for backstop liquidity.
insights.quote: you can see the trend is majority propm ... zeroi terrestrial and goonfi are around 80% 80 90% ... Midora um like generic pool model actually maintain around 10 to 20% of share

insights.claim: Prop AMMs need oracle prices from live traditional exchanges to function effectively, so during nights and weekends when oracle pricing quality drops, pool models pick up materially more share.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: Time-of-day market structure matters; pool-based DEXs may capture disproportionate value during off-hours when prop AMMs withdraw quotes.
insights.quote: when we when you go into like nighttime or weekends when there isn't I guess the same quality of Oracle pricing um you kind of have to let let the like defy market itself decide

insights.claim: Pool-model liquidity on tokenized equities risks being adverse-selected as toxic flow during market hours, similar to how SOL/USDC volume on traditional AMMs became stale-quote toxic flow once prop AMMs dominated.
insights.speaker: Carlos (guest)
insights.so_what: Passive pool LPs in equities may face losses from picking-off during open hours; the SOL/USDC precedent is the cautionary analogue.
insights.quote: the remaining sold USDC volume on traditional AMMs was just toxic flow in the sense that uh it was just like stale quotes that people were getting picked off on

insights.claim: Current DEX volume share is heavily distorted by incentives, points, and bonus programs, so present volume rankings may not be indicative of the long-run market structure outcome.
insights.speaker: Danny (host)
insights.so_what: Discount current volume-share leaderboards when picking winners; wait for incentive programs to roll off before drawing structural conclusions.
insights.quote: incentives kind of drive a lot of things within crypto ... so we could see volumes like maintain or continue to grow ... but it might not really be indicative of like the long run outcome

insights.claim: Equity perpetual futures traded about 11 billion last week versus 1.4 billion in spot equities, with SpaceX perps alone over 4 billion (30%+ of onchain equity perp volume), mirroring Binance's 6-10x futures-to-spot ratio.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Perps are ~8x the spot market, so the larger near-term monetization and flow opportunity in onchain equities is in derivatives, not spot.
insights.quote: while spot equities traded about 1.4 billion last week, um the equity perks traded 11 billion ... similar to how on Binance the future to spot volume ratio is generally 6 to 10x

insights.claim: If tokenized equity volume on Solana increases multiples from here, Solana's fully-diluted-to-rev (FDV/REV) multiple could look cheap on a forward basis.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Direct valuation argument for SOL: growing equity volume is incremental REV that current multiples may not yet price in.
insights.quote: if if tokenized equity volume on Salana is to increase multiples from here um perhaps the um FPB to to rev multiple could look a little cheat

insights.claim: Tokenized equity activity should be more sustainable than memecoins because there is always a thematic bull market within equities, lifting Solana's revenue durability and valuation.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: Argues equity flow gives Solana a more durable revenue base than reflexive meme cycles, supporting a structurally higher multiple.
insights.quote: with tokenized equities, like there's always some thematic within equities, there's always some theme ... the level of activity is going to start to become a lot more sustainable

insights.claim: The legacy Solana memecoin trading user base (Phantom, Axiom, etc., still doing ~0.5-1 billion weekly meme volume) has not yet shifted behavior toward equities, but converting that flow is the key trend to watch.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: The untapped upside is conversion of existing meme traders into equity traders; meme-to-equity volume migration is the leading indicator of the thesis playing out.
insights.quote: this kind of like legacy memecoin trading user base has not like shifted their behavior yet, but it's something I'll be watching

themes.theme: Front ends and tradeable equity plays

insights.claim: There is no legitimately sophisticated trading front end on Solana today, with apps like Axiom charging 50-100 bips net take rate on largely memecoin flow with poor execution, leaving a gap for JTX to launch a Bloomberg-terminal-class product (targeted July release).
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: A specific product/timing thesis: JTX could capture sophisticated equity execution flow at launch; July is the catalyst date to watch.
insights.quote: there's no really legitimate sophisticated front ends at the moment on Salana ... the net take rate is like 50 to 100 bips sometimes ... coupled with like poor execution

insights.claim: The first front end to jump fastest and best on a new onchain trading trend historically captures majority market share over the following 5-6 months, a pattern seen 4-5 times (Uniswap/Banana Gun, Photon/Bonkbot, Axiom).
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Base-rate framework arguing first-mover front ends on tokenized equities (e.g. JTX) could win the category if they ship a leading product first.
insights.quote: there are players that hop on that first fastest and best and over the following five, six months, they take a majority of the market share

insights.claim: Jupiter and Phantom will be much harder to displace than the market expects because entrenched user behavior rarely shifts even for a better product, as shown when Kamino Swap offered better trades a year and a half ago but saw minimal usage.
insights.speaker: Carlos (guest)
insights.so_what: Counters the JTX-takeover thesis; incumbents Jupiter and Phantom may retain share, tempering the upside case for challengers.
insights.quote: I think Jupiter will be much harder to displace than people think ... even if you create them a better product I think user behavior is incredibly hard to change

insights.claim: Jito (JTX/JTO) is a reasonable long if you are bullish Solana DEX volume, since even modest monetization of that volume makes its valuation work despite a sizable recent rally.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: A direct ticker call for expressing the onchain-equity-volume thesis, with the caveat that the recent rally must be contextualized.
insights.quote: if you're truly bullish Salana Dex volume and you assume they get a clip of that um it doesn't take that much really of monetization for the valuation to start to make sense

insights.claim: Pools could be a sleeper trade because prop AMMs won't want to quote the long tail of equities, leaving pool DEXs (Meteora, Radium) as the liquidity layer for the long tail before prop AMMs get comfortable.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest)
insights.so_what: Contrarian liquidity-layer play: pool DEXs may capture the long-tail equity listings that prop AMMs ignore; Meteora is the main backpack/Sunrise pool.
insights.quote: a lot of these profms aren't going to want to quote the very long tale of equities. Um, so I do feel like pools could come back in a big way

insights.claim: Radium has been left for dead by the market (priced near 2022 lows after losing meme share to Pump Swap) but tokenized assets have surpassed memes to become its second-largest revenue line, creating upside optionality.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: A specific deep-value/optionality long: Radium's revenue mix is rotating to tokenized equities while priced for the prior meme bust.
insights.quote: Radium in particular, I think market has likely left it for dead ... but as you show here, tokenized assets are now their second largest uh revenue lion item

insights.claim: The bull case for Radium is tempered because its current revenue is ~100x its all-time revenue level (inflated by high meme fees), and equity-trading fees will likely compress relative to memes, so volume growth must offset fee compression.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the valuation risk on the Radium long: a 100x revenue multiple plus equity fee compression means volume must scale a lot to justify it.
insights.quote: their revenues today relative to their all-time revenues is something like a 100x multiple ... the fees probably might even need to compress on the equity trading side of things

insights.claim: Bitcoin made new range lows beneath 60K with a stretch toward 75 cited, attributed by the team to 'paying for Sailor sins' (Strategy/Stretch overhang).
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Establishes the bearish price backdrop and links the drawdown directly to Strategy's preferred-issuance overhang.
insights.quote: Bitcoin just made new range lows uh beneath 60K. We see stretch down to 75 this morning ... seems like we're just paying for Sailor Sins

insights.claim: The weekly RSI on the NASDAQ/Bitcoin pair is the most overbought it has ever been, a signal that historically coincides with high-time-frame Bitcoin cycle lows and favorable BTC returns over the following two years.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: A technical contrarian buy signal: extreme NAS/BTC overbought readings have historically marked BTC cycle bottoms with strong 2-year forward returns.
insights.quote: whenever the weekly RSI on the NAS Bitcoin pair is is overbought, you know, in excess of 80 ... that tends to coincide with a very high time frame cycle low

insights.claim: Luke expects Bitcoin to outperform the NASDAQ over the next two to three years and for relative performance leadership to switch back to Bitcoin by year end.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: An explicit relative-value call (long BTC vs NASDAQ) with a year-end leadership-rotation timeframe.
insights.quote: I wouldn't be surprised to see Bitcoin outperform the NASDAQ over the next two to three years. And, uh, yeah, likely by year end, I think ... the relative performance switches back to Bitcoin as the leader
